title: Mid-cycle seat add led to prorated surcharge removing negotiated volume discount on consolidated invoice
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer SiftMail reports a charge on their March consolidated invoice that appears to negate their negotiated volume discount after adding 12 seats mid-cycle. The invoice shows a prorated "surcharge" line where per-seat pricing reverted to list price for the prorated portion rather than applying the customer's tiered volume discount.

Impact:
- Customer sees a $4,322 unexpected line item affecting month close and internal chargebacks.
- Customer concerned this will recur on future mid-cycle seat changes.

Environment:
- Enterprise org on reserved/dedicated plan with negotiated tier: 31+ seats => $1.80/seat; 11-30 seats => $2.10/seat.
- New seats added 2026-03-05T15:40:12Z (12 seats) via admin console. Billing cycle starts 1st of month.

Requested outcome:
- Clarify why prorate used list price for added seats instead of applying the negotiated tier once total seat count exceeded threshold mid-cycle.
- Provide provisional credit for the overcharge and confirm permanent fix to pricing-engine logic for mid-cycle seat tiering.

title: Configuring sustained vs burst quota for multiplexed ingestion workers to avoid 429s
preview:
Issue summary: Customer Arcadia Labs reports recurring 429 throttles during steady-state ingestion runs from a fleet of short-lived uploader workers. They see 429s even when token usage looks within expected monthly/rolling quotas.

Impact: Intermittent failed uploads (10-25% of requests during 1–2 hour ingestion windows), delays in downstream indexing, and elevated retry traffic causing secondary waves of throttles. No billing disputes opened, but customer is concerned about reliability for nightly syncs.

Environment: self_serve tenant in prod (us-east primary, eu-west failover). Workers spawn ephemeral API keys (lifetime ~5–10 minutes) and open 20–50 concurrent connections per worker process. Ingestion pattern: steady sustained throughput ~120 requests/s for 90–120 minutes, with microbursts when workers restart.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Create N ephemeral keys and fan out 300 worker processes across 3 hosts.
2) Each worker initiates 20 parallel streaming sessions to /v1/generate for small prompts (avg 64 tokens).
3) Start all workers within a 90 second window (simulates a deployment rollout).
4) Observe 429 responses beginning ~30s after start; persists intermittently for the ingestion window.

Expected behavior: Smooth sustained throughput with occasional short-lived bursts handled by token bucket bursting.

Actual behavior: Tenant-level quota tokens are exhausted and 429s appear despite per-key buckets showing available tokens for some keys. 429s correlate with worker restarts and the fanout of ephemeral keys.
text:
Issue summary: Customer Arcadia Labs reports recurring 429 throttles during steady-state ingestion runs from a fleet of short-lived uploader workers. They see 429s even when token usage looks within expected monthly/rolling quotas.

Impact: Intermittent failed uploads (10-25% of requests during 1–2 hour ingestion windows), delays in downstream indexing, and elevated retry traffic causing secondary waves of throttles. No billing disputes opened, but customer is concerned about reliability for nightly syncs.

Environment: self_serve tenant in prod (us-east primary, eu-west failover). Workers spawn ephemeral API keys (lifetime ~5–10 minutes) and open 20–50 concurrent connections per worker process. Ingestion pattern: steady sustained throughput ~120 requests/s for 90–120 minutes, with microbursts when workers restart.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Create N ephemeral keys and fan out 300 worker processes across 3 hosts.
2) Each worker initiates 20 parallel streaming sessions to /v1/generate for small prompts (avg 64 tokens).
3) Start all workers within a 90 second window (simulates a deployment rollout).
4) Observe 429 responses beginning ~30s after start; persists intermittently for the ingestion window.

Expected behavior: Smooth sustained throughput with occasional short-lived bursts handled by token bucket bursting.

Actual behavior: Tenant-level quota tokens are exhausted and 429s appear despite per-key buckets showing available tokens for some keys. 429s correlate with worker restarts and the fanout of ephemeral keys.

title: Ephemeral connection spike triggering GPU attach backoff and mid-window throughput sag in dedicated pool
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer NimbleAI reports a mid-day p50/p99 throughput drop in their dedicated pool. Investigations show a burst of short-lived connections from a small tenant group coincided with repeated GPU attach failures and exponential attach backoff from the allocator, leading to effective slot starvation and reduced batched throughput.

Impact:
- Customer-facing endpoints experienced ~30% drop in throughput and p99 increased from 280ms to 1.2s for 20 minutes during the spike window.
- Requests were not returning 5xx but observed long queue times and autoscaler reacted with rapid scale-down/scale-up cycles (oscillation).

Environment:
- Dedicated pool for NimbleAI (reserved GPUs), prod - us-east-1
- Serving runtime v3.8.1, autoscaler v2.4.0, sidecar v1.9.

Timeline (local):
- 2026-03-12 11:46 UTC: Gradual p99 drift begins.
- 2026-03-12 11:52 UTC: Connection spike (short-lived streams) observed from tenant group ID T-9843.
- 2026-03-12 11:53–12:10 UTC: Repeated attach failures surfaced, attach backoff increased to 45s.
- 2026-03-12 12:15 UTC: Autoscaler triggered two shrink/grow cycles.
- 2026-03-12 12:25 UTC: Traffic normalizes after short-term mitigations.

Steps to reproduce (approx):
1) Simulate many short streaming connections (0.5s–2s) with high fanout on a single tenant.
2) Observe allocator logs for repeated \"attach_timeout\" and increasing backoff intervals.
3) Watch autoscaler metrics for number_of_slots and reconcile loop frequency.

Logs / Metrics observed:
- allocator.log: repeated \"gpu_attach_timeout: device busy\" entries from 11:52–12:10 UTC.
- autoscaler: scale decisions at 11:54 and 12:11 with immediate opposite decision within 60s.
- serving-runtime: queue_time p50 up 35%, p99 up 4x.
- sidecar: high conn churn metric (connections/sec) spike ~8x baseline.

Temporary mitigations applied by Support:
- Blackholed problematic tenant T-9843 connections at edge for 9 minutes (reduced churn).
- Increased allocator attach retries from 3->6 for the affected pool (mitigated attach backoff tail).
- Pinned NimbleAI to a warmed reservation to avoid cold attach pressure during window.

Current ask / next steps:
- Engineering to review allocator attach backoff policy and identify why short-lived connection bursts lead to backoff amplification.
- Evaluate sidecar connection throttling to prevent single-tenant fanout from saturating attach path.
- Consider autoscaler hysteresis increase to avoid oscillatory shrink/grow from transient churn.

title: Traffic diverted to standby variant causing long-tail latency and intermittent 504s for enterprise billing service
preview: Issue summary: Starting ~2026-03-13 10:20 UTC NimbusFinance reported elevated end-to-end latency and intermittent 504s on their nightly billing job. Impact: 30% of billing requests experienced >5s latency and ~6% returned 504s over a 2 hour window, causing downstream reconciliation failures and customer alerts. Environment: Dedicated tenant (NimbusFinance) on prod-us-east, routing policy: cost-aware primary->standby diversion enabled for load smoothing. Observed behavior: Traffic was routed to "standby" variant replicas that reported as READY but were still performing model KV population and kernel JIT; this produced serial warmups and long-tail p99. The issue appears tied to a routing decision that prefers standby racks when observed primary CPU pressure exceeded a small threshold.
text: Issue summary: Starting ~2026-03-13 10:20 UTC NimbusFinance reported elevated end-to-end latency and intermittent 504s on their nightly billing job. Impact: 30% of billing requests experienced >5s latency and ~6% returned 504s over a 2 hour window, causing downstream reconciliation failures and customer alerts. Environment: Dedicated tenant (NimbusFinance) on prod-us-east, routing policy: cost-aware primary->standby diversion enabled for load smoothing. Observed behavior: Traffic was routed to "standby" variant replicas that reported as READY but were still performing model KV population and kernel JIT; this produced serial warmups and long-tail p99. The issue appears tied to a routing decision that prefers standby racks when observed primary CPU pressure exceeded a small threshold.

title: Device-trust approval delay triggers repeated console access-request loop
preview:
Issue summary:
A subset of internal users attempting to sign in to the Redwood Console are stuck in an access-request loop: the console shows "Waiting for device approval" and the platform repeatedly creates new pending access-request records instead of updating the existing request. Approvers are not receiving stable notifications, and on-call pages are being generated for duplicate requests.

Impact:
- ~12 engineers across two squads reported inability to complete console login via SSO (device approval required).
- Flood of duplicate access-request tickets in the admin queue causing approver overload and delayed approvals.
- Temporary manual approvals required for critical oncall responders. No production compute or model serving impact observed.

Environment:
- Prod sign-in flow through Okta + Device Trust + internal SSO bridge (sso-bridge). Region: us-west. Affected users appear to be on iOS-managed devices and a few macOS devices with the latest MDM profile.

Steps to reproduce (observed):
1. User attempts console sign-in via Okta SSO from managed device.
2. Okta returns device-challenge required -> device-trust-service triggers an approval request to the approver queue.
3. The user approves on-device; device-trust service signals success, but sso-bridge polls the approval endpoint and receives intermittent 202 / empty payload due to a race with the approval indexer.
4. sso-bridge treats the response as "no approved record" and re-creates a new access-request entry (new request_id), producing duplicate pending items.
5. Approver webhooks and email notifications are rate-limited and deduplicated imperfectly, so notifications are inconsistent.

Observed logs (snippets):
- sso-bridge.log: "[2026-03-11T14:22:03Z] warn: approval-poll no-record for device-req token=abc123 polling=3"
- device-trust-service.log: "[2026-03-11T14:22:02Z] info: approval indexer wrote request_id=R-20260311-7"
- auth-proxy.log: "[2026-03-11T14:22:04Z] error: duplicate-access-request created new id R-20260311-8 for user=j.lee@example.com"

Hypothesis:
- A race condition between the approval indexer and the sso-bridge polling leads to a transient 202/no-body response that sso-bridge treats as "not approved" and creates a new request instead of waiting/backing off.
- Notification/dedup logic in admin-queue assumes stable request IDs and does not correlate repeated requests for the same user/device.

Immediate mitigation (already applied):
- Manual approval of critical oncall requests by SRE to restore login access.
- Temporarily increased visibility: added a dashboard query to show access-request creation rate and duplicate_count per user.

Proposed fixes / next steps:
- Short-term (hotfix): Update sso-bridge polling to interpret 202/no-body as a retryable transient and implement exponential backoff + idempotency key lookup before creating a new request. (PR planned).
- Medium-term: Harden approval indexer to emit a pre-write lease or a transient marker so pollers can detect in-progress writes.
- Long-term: Improve admin notification deduplication by matching on user+device fingerprint and recent requests (rolling window).

Attachments / references:
- Slack thread: #support (https://redwood.slack.com/archives/C0123456)
- Runbook: https://confluence.redwood.internal/display/OPS/Device+Trust+Runbook

title: Duplicate final choice and lingering partial tokens after mobile app background/reconnect (WebSocket)
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer ClipNote reports that when their mobile app (iOS) backgrounds and then reconnects over WebSocket, the final choice from the model is sometimes duplicated and a short sequence of partial tokens reappears after the UI believes the response is finished. This occurs sporadically and appears correlated with mobile network transitions (Wi-Fi -> cellular) and device sleep/resume.

Impact:
- Affects production users of ClipNote mobile app (estimated ~0.6% of sessions during last 48h).
- Duplicated final output leads to confusing UI (repeated sentence) and occasionally triggers client-side logic that posts the same message twice.
- Customer classifies as P1 / Sev1 due to visible UX regressions on their paid tier.

Environment:
- Customer tier: enterprise (dedicated capacity).
- Region: us-east (cluster rwi-prod-1).
- Mobile: iOS 15/16, app uses Redwood mobile SDK v0.9.3 (WebSocket transport).
- Proxy: customer sits behind an edge proxy with short NAT idle timeouts (~30s).

Steps to reproduce (customer-provided):
1) Start a streaming generation from the ClipNote mobile app (WebSocket).
2) Allow the device to auto-lock or manually background the app (~10-60s).
3) Move device between Wi-Fi and cellular or wait for NAT to expire, then foreground the app so the SDK auto-reconnects the same conversation.
4) Observe the transcript: sometimes the final sentence is emitted twice and a 2-6 token fragment appears after the end event that matches the tail of the previous tokens.

Observed behavior:
- Server logs show a normal final 'done' event for the original connection.
- On reconnect, the SDK performs a resume by resending the last seen frame index. The reconnect session sometimes emits a short fragment (2-6 tokens) that duplicates the tail of the previous choice, then emits a terminal event again.
- Packet captures show the old TCP connection closed by NAT; a new TCP/TLS handshake and WS upgrade follow. There is a narrow window where both server-side streaming buffer and client-side resume logic appear to output the same final fragment twice.

Expected behavior:
- After a successful resume the client should not receive duplicate tokens from the server and the final-choice should only be delivered once.

Logs and supporting artifacts:
- Customer-provided client logs (attached) show resume index: 1234, server ack for last_frame: 1234 followed by final choice.
- Server-side excerpt (rwi-prod-1, stream_id=clipnote-55819): shows final chunk and 'stream_end' event at 2026-03-09T21:12:08Z.
- Packet capture (pcap) indicates server's final chunk left the socket but client NAT teardown caused retransmit window that led to resume handshake being processed by a different proxy worker.

Initial hypothesis:
1) Race between server emitting final end event and client resume causing the runtime to produce a short duplicate fragment when a reconnection maps to a different worker that still has buffered partial output.
2) Idempotency/sequence-state not being validated across resume when reconnection lands on a different serving-runtime instance behind the proxy.
3) The mobile SDK's auto-resume may resubmit the final request fragment without strict de-dup checks, allowing duplicated tokens to reappear in UI.

Immediate workaround suggested to customer:
- Disable automatic resume on app foreground and instead perform a full new request on app resume (client-side change). This avoids duplicate fragments at expense of re-generation.
- If possible, increase proxy NAT timeout to reduce frequency of early disconnects (edge fix).

Next steps (investigation):
- SRE: reproduce locally using network throttling and NAT timeout simulation with mobile SDK emulator (assigned to Jordan Blake).
- Engineering: add additional server-side dedupe by final_choice_id in the streaming proxy and include 'final_choice_id' in end events for cross-worker dedupe (proposal to be drafted).
- SDK: add an optional 'resume_strategy' toggle that disables auto-replay of last fragment and opts for explicit dedupe logic.

Reference artifacts/links:
- Slack thread: #support-clipnote (https://slack.redwood.com/archives/C0234ABC/clipnote-resume-dup)

title: Provision 'Model Safety Review' pod: Confluence spaces, delegated admin groups, and timezone-aware ephemeral Slack standup channels
preview:
Issue summary:
We need a standardized project bootstrap for the new cross-functional 'Model Safety Review' pods. The request covers creating scoped Confluence spaces with templated pages, provisioning delegated Confluence admin groups per pod, and creating a set of ephemeral Slack channels for async/standup coordination with timezone-aware naming and archival automation.

Background and rationale:
- Several new pods will start working on safety evaluations and need consistent docs and low-friction collaboration spaces.
- Current ad-hoc creation leads to inconsistent permissions, missing watchers, and manual cleanup.
- Pods operate across APAC/EMEA/NA timezones; standup channels should be grouped by timezone to reduce noise.

Requested deliverables (high level):
1) Create a Confluence parent space named "Model Safety Review - <pod-name>" for each new pod (template to be applied). The space should have the following: a pre-populated "Evaluation Runbook" page, an "Artifacts & Results" page, and a "Decision Log" page.
2) Provision Confluence groups per pod: safety-<pod-name>-admins (Confluence admin role scoped to the space), safety-<pod-name>-viewers. Add initial users (list below) to appropriate groups.
3) Create Slack channels for each pod using naming pattern: #msr-<pod-name>-standup-<tz> (where <tz> is na / eme / apac). Channels must be created as private channels and have pinned templates for standup prompts.
4) Add automation: a) Add watchers (safety-<pod-name>-viewers) to the Confluence space; b) Configure a scheduled archival policy: Slack channels auto-archive after 90 days of inactivity; Confluence space set to be flagged for review after 180 days with a one-click archive button for platform ops.
5) Wire a simple Slack-to-Confluence bridge for standup summaries: a daily summary message posted to the space's "Daily Standups" page via the existing integration bot.

Initial pod list and membership to provision now:
- pod-name: firebreak-eval -> admins: [Marcus Kim, Lena Ortiz] -> viewers: [Anita Shah, Priya Rao, Carlos Mendes]
- pod-name: redwood-adversarial -> admins: [Jordan Blake] -> viewers: [Marcus Kim, Elena Park]

Acceptance criteria (see separate field for clarity): space templates exist, groups created and scoped, channels created with correct naming/visibility, automations configured and tested, owners notified.

title: Hosted API: intermittent 504s / request timeouts on chat completions (us-east)
preview:
Issue summary:
- Customer reports 504 gateway timeouts on /v1/chat/completions.

Impact:
- End users see timeouts after ~30s, retries sometimes succeed.
- Approx error rate reported by customer: 2-5% over last hour.

Primary symptom: Request timeouts
Deployment offering: Hosted API

Environment:
- prod, us-east
- model: rw-chat-3.1

Initial triage notes:
- Correlates with elevated queue depth on shared gateway pool.
- No auth failures observed; looks like upstream request expiry.

Links:
- Slack: https://slack.com/app_redirect?channel=support (thread referenced in comments)

title: RRB-17: Private installer rollback loop / stuck rollback during upgrade
preview:
Issue summary:
Customers upgrading Redwood Private 2.8.0 -> 2.9.0 can enter an infinite rollback retry loop. UI shows "Rollback in progress" and never completes.

Impact:
- Upgrade/rollback does not converge; customer cluster remains in degraded state.
- Support load + customer trust risk.

Environment:
- Private installer 2.9.0
- Kubernetes (various), observed in us-east + eu-west

Suspected root cause:
Rollback cleanup hook exits before clearing rollback lock (ConfigMap `installer-rollback-lock`), causing controller to retry indefinitely.

Workaround (customer-safe):
1) Confirm namespace `redwood-private`
2) `kubectl -n redwood-private delete cm installer-rollback-lock`
3) `kubectl -n redwood-private rollout restart deploy/installer-controller`

Acceptance criteria / fix:
- Always best-effort clear lock
- Add TTL + loop detection to stop retries and surface actionable status
- Add metric + alert when rollback retries >3 in 10m

Comments:
- 2026-02-12 Jordan Blake: Related to incident INC-2147. See Slack #incidents thread_ts=1770937323.
- 2026-02-13 Priya Natarajan: Patch in progress; will land in 2.9.1. PR: redwood-private-installer#842.
- 2026-02-14 Dev Patel: Added metric `installer.rollback.loop_detected` and proposed alert threshold.

title: Scheduler queue buildup delays streaming start times (Hosted us-west)
preview:
Issue summary:
First token times for streaming are extremely high; queue depth climbing. Some clients retry aggressively, compounding load.

Impact:
Customer-visible latency; timeouts at gateway.

Environment:
prod us-west; hosted tier.

Notes:
Suspected regression in scheduler admission control under high concurrency.
text:
Issue summary:

Impact:
Customer-visible latency; timeouts at gateway.

Environment:
prod us-west; hosted tier.

Notes:
Suspected regression in scheduler admission control under high concurrency.

title: Dedicated: streaming resets when fallback routing triggers mid-request
preview: Customer on Dedicated reports that during capacity pressure, some streaming requests terminate early. They noticed it coincides with fallback routing events in their logs (model pin not strict).
text: Customer on Dedicated reports that during capacity pressure, some streaming requests terminate early. They noticed it coincides with fallback routing events in their logs (model pin not strict).

title: Orion Defense requesting offline installer bundle for closed network Private deployment
preview:
Issue summary
Orion Defense cannot allow any outbound internet access from their Kubernetes cluster (closed enclave). They are requesting an offline installer bundle (images + charts) and a checklist documenting any outbound assumptions.

Impact
Blocks Private proof-of-concept until packaging is available for disconnected install.

Environment
On-prem Kubernetes, internal-only DNS/NTP, internal PKI. Public OCSP/CRL not permitted.

Customer request
- Provide container images in a format they can import into internal registry.
- Provide pinned digests + checksums.
- Provide upgrade cadence expectations (quarterly windows).

Comments
2025-02-14 Jordan Blake (reporter): Customer says "no outbound" and "no proxy". Need to make sure nothing phones home.
2025-02-15 Hiro Tanaka (assignee): We can generate a v0 bundle from the current private-installer release. Will include digest list + checksums.
2025-02-18 Priya Natarajan: Please validate install with egress blocked at firewall, not just DNS. Also include note about disabling telemetry.
2025-02-21 Hiro Tanaka: Delivered bundle via secure transfer. Orion confirmed they can import images to internal registry. Added checklist link and scheduled follow-up security review.

Resolution
Provided offline bundle + checklist. No code changes required for POC; documentation updates tracked separately.

title: Go SDK reports 'invalid API key' when key loaded from file includes trailing newline
preview: Issue summary: Customer running Redwood Private deployment reports Go SDK errors 'invalid API key' after moving from env var injection to reading key from a mounted file. They confirmed the key works via curl when manually copied. Impact: app cannot authenticate to private control plane. Steps: (1) read file contents (2) pass string to redwood.NewClient(apiKey) (3) API returns 401 with message invalid_api_key. Hypothesis: key string contains trailing newline or spaces.
text: Issue summary: Customer running Redwood Private deployment reports Go SDK errors 'invalid API key' after moving from env var injection to reading key from a mounted file. They confirmed the key works via curl when manually copied. Impact: app cannot authenticate to private control plane. Steps: (1) read file contents (2) pass string to redwood.NewClient(apiKey) (3) API returns 401 with message invalid_api_key. Hypothesis: key string contains trailing newline or spaces.
